subject: Chemistry
q: Stability of the species  <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>Li</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msub> <mtext>,</mtext> <mspace width="0.5em"></mspace> <msubsup> <mtext>Li</mtext> <mn>2</mn> <mo>-</mo> </msubsup> <mspace width="0.5em"></mspace> <mtext>and</mtext> <mspace width="0.5em"></mspace> <msubsup> <mtext>Li</mtext> <mn>2</mn> <mo>+</mo> </msubsup> </mrow> </math> increases in the order of
question: Stability of the species  <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>Li</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msub> <mtext>,</mtext> <mspace width="0.5em"></mspace> <msubsup> <mtext>Li</mtext> <mn>2</mn> <mo>-</mo> </msubsup> <mspace width="0.5em"></mspace> <mtext>and</mtext> <mspace width="0.5em"></mspace> <msubsup> <mtext>Li</mtext> <mn>2</mn> <mo>+</mo> </msubsup> </mrow> </math> increases in the order of
solution:
Li 2 - < Li 2 + < Li 2
Bond order ∝ stability of species
Li2=σ1s2<σ*1s2<σ2s2                      ;  B.O=4−22=1
Li2−=σ1s2<σ*1s2<σ2s2<σ*2s1                      ;  B.O=4−32=0.5
Li2+=σ1s2<σ*1s2<σ2s1                      ;  B.O=3−22=0.5
Li2+ and Li2- both have same bond order, but Li2- has more number of electron in anti-bonding molecular orbitals (experience more inter electronic repulsion) and hence least stable among three species.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : A π bonding MO has lower electron density above and below the inter-nuclear axis.

Statement (II) : The π* antibonding MO has a node between the nuclei.

In light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : A π bonding MO has lower electron density above and below the inter-nuclear axis.

Statement (II) : The π* antibonding MO has a node between the nuclei.

In light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution:
The pi bonding molecular orbital has a larger electron density above and below the internuclear axis due to the symmetry of the orbital, interference of atomic orbitals, and overlap of atomic orbitals. The electron density is minimum along the internuclear axis due to the nodal plane.

A π bonding molecular orbital has higher electron density above and below inter nuclear axis.

This π* orbital has two nodes: one node is the plane which contains the atoms, and the other node is a plane perpendicular to this, between the two carbon atoms.

subject: Chemistry
q: According to Lewis theory, the total number of $\sigma$ bond-pairs and lone pair of electrons around the central atom of $XeO_6^{4-}$ ion is _____.
question: According to Lewis theory, the total number of $\sigma$ bond-pairs and lone pair of electrons around the central atom of $XeO_6^{4-}$ ion is _____.
solution:
The central atom in $XeO_6^{4-}$ is Xenon (Xe).

The number of valence electrons in Xe is $8$.

Let the oxidation state of Xe in $XeO_6^{4-}$ be $x$.

$x + 6(-2) = -4$

$x = +8$

Since the oxidation state of Xe is $+8$, all $8$ of its valence electrons are involved in bonding with the $6$ oxygen atoms. There are no non-bonding electrons left on the central atom.

Number of lone pairs on Xe $= 0$

Xe forms $6$ $\sigma$ bonds with the $6$ oxygen atoms (and $2$ $\pi$ bonds to satisfy its $+8$ oxidation state and minimize formal charge).

Number of $\sigma$ bond-pairs $= 6$

Total number of $\sigma$ bond-pairs and lone pairs around the central atom $= 6 + 0 = 6$.

Answer: $6$

options: greater than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> but less than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>373</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>
solution: At room temperature water is liquid and has boiling points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>373</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> due to hydrogen bonding. Where as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math> is gas and it has no hydrogen bonding. Hence boiling point of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math> is less than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> [Boiling point of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>60</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>]

q: Which of the following species exhibits the diamagnetic behaviour?
question: Which of the following species exhibits the diamagnetic behaviour?
solution:
The correct option is $\mathrm{O}_2^{2-}$
This species has $18 \mathrm{~e}^{-}$, which are filled in such a way that all molecular orbitals are fully filled, so diamagnetic.
$\mathrm{\sigma 1 s^2 \sigma^* 1 s^2, \sigma 2 s^2 \sigma^* 2 s^2, \sigma 2 p_z^2, \pi 2 p_x^2=\pi 2 p_y^2, \pi^* 2 p_x^2=\pi^* 2 p_y^2}$
Hence, (A) is correct.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I : SO2 and H2O both possess V-shaped structure

Statement II : The bond angle of SO2 is less than that of H2O.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I : SO2 and H2O both possess V-shaped structure

Statement II : The bond angle of SO2 is less than that of H2O.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
solution:
The molecule SO2 is having two sigma bond pairs and lone pair, hence, it exhibits angular or V-shape structure. The molecule H2O is having two sigma bond pairs and a lone pair hence, it exhibits angular or V-shape structure. So, both molecules are having same shape. But double bond-double bond repulsions are greater than single bond repulsions, hence, the bond angle in sulphur dioxide is greater than water.

SO2 and H2O both have V-Shape 

Bond angle: SO2 > H2O

q:
Bonding in which of the following diatomic molecule(s) become(s) stronger, on the basis of MO Theory, by removal of an electron?

(A) NO

(B) N2

(C) O2

(D) C2

(E) B2

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Bonding in which of the following diatomic molecule(s) become(s) stronger, on the basis of MO Theory, by removal of an electron?

(A) NO

(B) N2

(C) O2

(D) C2

(E) B2

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
solution: In all these molecules O2​ and NO, is stabilized by removal of electrons because it is having electrons in the anti-bonding orbital. And thus removal of electron from the anti bonding orbital, increases the bond order of O2 and NO​. And thus is becomes more stable after removal of electrons. Bond order of O2+​is 2.5 and it is more than that of O2​ which is having bond order as 2. Similarly Bond order of NO is 2.5 and that of NO+ is 3.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List - I with List-II

  List I (Molecule)   List II (Shape)
A. BrF5   i. T-shape
B. H2O ii. See saw
C. ClF3 iii. Bent
D. SF4 iv. Square pyramidal
question:
Match List - I with List-II

  List I (Molecule)   List II (Shape)
A. BrF5   i. T-shape
B. H2O ii. See saw
C. ClF3 iii. Bent
D. SF4 iv. Square pyramidal
solution:
(A) The steric number of the central Bromine atom is =5 bond pairs+one lone pair =6. Hence the shape is square pyramidal structure and the hybridisation is sp3d2.

(B) H2O molecular geometry is bent because it has two bound atoms and two lone pairs on oxygen, the central atom. The two lone pairs on the oxygen atom push the two hydrogen atoms down further away from the oxygen atom and closer to each other, creating a bent shape and 104.5 degrees angle between the two H atoms.

(C) The molecular geometry of ClF3 is said to be T-shaped. ClF3 acquires such a shape because two lone pairs take up equatorial positions as they require more space and greater repulsions. They are arranged in a trigonal bipyramidal shape with 3 bonds and 2 lone pairs.

(D)  SF4 has an sp3d type of hybridisation. In a molecule of SF4, each S atom is bonded to four fluorine atoms.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Among the following, the CORRECT combinations are
A. $\mathrm{IF}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{~T}$-shaped (sp$\left.{ }^{3} \mathrm{~d}\right)$
B. $\mathrm{IF}_{5} \rightarrow$ Square pyramidal $\left(\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}\right)$
C. $\mathrm{IF}_{7} \rightarrow$ Pentagonal bipyramidal $\left(\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{3}\right)$
D. $\mathrm{ClO}_{4}{ }^{-} \rightarrow$ Square planar $\left(\mathrm{sp}^{2} \mathrm{~d}\right)$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Among the following, the CORRECT combinations are
A. $\mathrm{IF}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{~T}$-shaped (sp$\left.{ }^{3} \mathrm{~d}\right)$
B. $\mathrm{IF}_{5} \rightarrow$ Square pyramidal $\left(\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}\right)$
C. $\mathrm{IF}_{7} \rightarrow$ Pentagonal bipyramidal $\left(\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{3}\right)$
D. $\mathrm{ClO}_{4}{ }^{-} \rightarrow$ Square planar $\left(\mathrm{sp}^{2} \mathrm{~d}\right)$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Analyzing geometry and hybridization:

A. IF₃: Iodine has 7 valence electrons, forms 3 bonds with F, leaving 2 lone pairs. Total electron pairs = 5 (trigonal bipyramidal geometry). Molecular shape is T-shaped. Hybridization = sp³d. CORRECT.

B. IF₅: Iodine forms 5 bonds with F and has 1 lone pair. Total electron pairs = 6 (octahedral geometry). Molecular shape is square pyramidal with 1 lone pair in axial position. Hybridization = sp³d². CORRECT.

C. IF₇: Iodine forms 7 bonds with F and has 0 lone pairs. Total electron pairs = 7. Molecular shape is pentagonal bipyramidal. Hybridization = sp³d³. CORRECT.

D. ClO₄⁻: Chlorine has 7 valence electrons + 1 from charge = 8. Bonded to 4 O atoms with 0 lone pairs. Total electron pairs = 4 (tetrahedral geometry). Molecular shape is tetrahedral, NOT square planar. Hybridization = sp³, NOT sp²d. INCORRECT.

Correct combinations: A, B, and C.

subject: Chemistry
q: Number of molecules having bond order 2 from the following molecules is ________ \(\mathrm{C}_2, \mathrm{O}_2, \mathrm{Be}_2, \mathrm{Li}_2, \mathrm{Ne}_2, \mathrm{~N}_2, \mathrm{He}_2\)
question: Number of molecules having bond order 2 from the following molecules is ________ \(\mathrm{C}_2, \mathrm{O}_2, \mathrm{Be}_2, \mathrm{Li}_2, \mathrm{Ne}_2, \mathrm{~N}_2, \mathrm{He}_2\)
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_2 \\ & \left(12 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right): \sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma 2 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}^2\left[\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^2=\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}^2\right] \\ & \text { B.O. }=\frac{8-4}{2}=2 \\ & \mathrm{O}_2 \\ & \left(16 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right): \sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma 2 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma 2 \mathrm{pz}^2 \\ & {\left[\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^2=\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}^2\right]\left[\pi * 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^1=\pi * 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}^1\right]} \\ & \text { B.O. }=\frac{10-6}{2}=2 \\ & \text { Be }{ }_2 \\ & \left(8 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right): \sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma 2 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}^2 \\ & \text { B.O. }=\frac{4-4}{2}=0 \\ & \mathrm{Li}_2 \\ & \left(6 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right): \sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma 2 \mathrm{~s}^2\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { B.O. }=\frac{4-2}{2}=1 \\ & \mathrm{Ne}_2 \\ & \left(20 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right): \sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma 2 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma 2 \mathrm{pz}^2 \\ & {\left[\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^2=\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}^2\right]\left[\pi * 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^2=\pi^* 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}^2\right] \sigma^* 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{z}}^2} \\ & \text { B.O. }=\frac{10-10}{2}=0 \\ & \mathrm{~N}_2 \\ & \left(14 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right): \sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma 2 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}^2\left[\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^2=\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}^2\right] \sigma 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{z}}^2 \\ & \text { B.O. }=\frac{10-4}{2}=6 \\ & \mathrm{He}_2 \\ & \left(4 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right): \sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^2, \sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}^2 \\ & \text { B.O. }=\frac{2-2}{2}=0\end{aligned}$

q: A metal complex with a formula $\mathrm{MC} \ell_4 \cdot 3 \mathrm{NH}_3$ is involved in $\mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{~d}^2$ hybridisation. It upon reaction with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$ solution gives ' x ' moles of AgCl. Consider ' x ' is equal to the number of lone pairs of electron present in central atom of $\mathrm{BrF}_5$. Then the number of geometrical isomers exhibited by the complex is ______.
question: A metal complex with a formula $\mathrm{MC} \ell_4 \cdot 3 \mathrm{NH}_3$ is involved in $\mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{~d}^2$ hybridisation. It upon reaction with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$ solution gives ' x ' moles of AgCl. Consider ' x ' is equal to the number of lone pairs of electron present in central atom of $\mathrm{BrF}_5$. Then the number of geometrical isomers exhibited by the complex is ______.
solution:
1 lone pair hence 1 mole AgCl.
Complex is $\left[\mathrm{M}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{Cl}_3\right] \mathrm{Cl}$.
It shows 2 geometrical isomers $\left(\mathrm{Ma}_3 \mathrm{~b}_3\right.$ type) facial (fac) & meridional (Mer)

q:
Consider, PF5,BrF5,PCl3,SF6,ICl4-,ClF3 and IF5

Amongst the above molecule(s) ion(s), the number of molecule(s)/ion(s) having sp3d2 hybridisation is
question:
Consider, PF5,BrF5,PCl3,SF6,ICl4-,ClF3 and IF5

Amongst the above molecule(s) ion(s), the number of molecule(s)/ion(s) having sp3d2 hybridisation is
solution:
Number of hybrid orbitals = Number of bond pair + number of lone pair on central atom.

To have sp3d2 hybridisation there should be total of 6 (bond pairs + lone pairs). The structure in such case will be octahedral or its derivative structures.

subject: Chemistry
q: The number of paramagnetic species among the following is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>Li</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup><mo>,</mo><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msubsup><mo>,</mo><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></msubsup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>He</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></msubsup></math>
question: The number of paramagnetic species among the following is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>Li</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup><mo>,</mo><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msubsup><mo>,</mo><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></msubsup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>He</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></msubsup></math>
solution:
The species that contain unpaired electrons are paramagnetic.

Electronic configuration of Li2 molecule is σ1 s2σ*1 s2σ2 s2.

The electronic configuration of B2 molecule is

σ1s2σ⋆1s2σ2s2σ⋆2s2π2px1=π2py1

The presence of unpaired electrons in the two π bonding molecular orbitals explains its paramagnetic nature.

The electronic configuration of C2  KKσ2s2σ*2s2π2px2π2py2

O2: KKσ2 s2σ2 s2σ2pz2π2px2=π2py2π*2px1=π*2py1

Hence, O2 molecule is paramagnetic.

The electronic configuration of C2- is σ1s2σ⋆1s2σ2s2σ⋆2s2π2px2=π2py2σ2pz1

Hence, it is paramagnetic substance.

O22-: KKσ2 s2σ2 s2σ2pz2π2px2=π2py2π*2px2=π*2py2

O2+: KKσ2 s2σ2 s2σ2pz2π2px2=π2py2π*2px1=π*2py

Hence, the above species is paramagnetic.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Amongst the following the number of oxide(s) which are paramagnetic in nature is

Na2O,KO2,NO2, N2O,ClO2,NO,SO2,Cl2O
question:
Amongst the following the number of oxide(s) which are paramagnetic in nature is

Na2O,KO2,NO2, N2O,ClO2,NO,SO2,Cl2O
solution:
The magnetic properties of a substance can be determined by examining its electron configuration: If it has unpaired electrons, then the substance is paramagnetic and if all electrons are paired, the substance is then diamagnetic.

KO2 is potassium superoxide. There is one unpaired electron in anti-bonding molecular orbital of superoxide. Potassium superoxide is paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired electron.

NO2,ClO2,NO are odd electron species. Hence, they are paramagnetic.

The other are monoxide and dioxide contain paired electrons, so they will show diamagnetic behaviour.

subject: Chemistry
q: According to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MO</mi></math> theory the bond orders for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>NO</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> respectively, are
question: According to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MO</mi></math> theory the bond orders for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>NO</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> respectively, are
solution:
(i) Molecular orbital configuration of O22- is KK (σ2s)2 (σ*2s)2 𝜋(2px)2 𝜋(2py)2 (σ2pz)2 𝜋*(2px)2 𝜋*(2py)2

Bond order = 12 (Nb - Na) = 12 8 - 6 = 1

(ii) Molecular orbital configuration of CO is KK (σ2s)2 (σ*2s)2 𝜋(2px)2 𝜋(2py)2 (σ2pz)2 

Bond order = 12 (Nb - Na) = 12 8 - 2 = 3

(iii) Molecular orbital configuration of NO+ is KK (σ2s)2 (σ*2s)2 𝜋(2px)2 𝜋(2py)2 (σ2pz)2 

Bond order = 12 (Nb - Na) = 12 8 - 2 = 3

So, according to molecular orbital theory, the bond orders respectively are 1, 3 and 3.

subject: Chemistry
q: The element that shows greater ability to form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pπ</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>pπ</mi></math> multiple bonds is:
question: The element that shows greater ability to form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pπ</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>pπ</mi></math> multiple bonds is:
solution: <math><mi>C</mi></math> forms most stable <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pπ</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>pπ</mi></math> bonds because it has high bond dissociation energy, more electronegativity, high ionization energy and vacant <math><mi>d</mi><mo>-</mo></math> orbital is unavailable. While others have vacant orbital and low bond dissociation enthalpy and low ionisation enthalpy
